# Questions in Livestream 160

Live Stream #160: The Synchronicity Of 13,000 Subs

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=453" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:33</a> | Will modern English translations age the way the King James Version did? | He says yes, because language shifts over time and even comparatively recent translations already feel dated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=845" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:05</a> | Are there proto-mummies that show how Egyptian mummification developed, or why Egyptians started the process? | He says mummification first happened naturally in dry sand burials, and intentional mummification tried to recreate that natural preservation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=995" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:35</a> | Did Moses know before fleeing Egypt that his family line would become future rulers? | He says the text does not tell us, though Moses may have known he had been adopted into Egypt's highest governing circles. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=1109" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:29</a> | What is his favorite part of Cecil B. DeMille's Ten Commandments? | He says the Egyptian dancing scene, because the dance movements are unusually historically accurate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=1239" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:39</a> | Does Isaiah 3:12 settle egalitarian debates? | He says the passage is not a simple modern proof text and needs to be read in its own context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=1543" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:43</a> | Did he watch the Gavin Ortlund and Trent Horn dialogue with Kip and Bowen, and what were his thoughts? | He says the setup was skewed by the moderator, the central slavery objection was never really argued, and Ortlund and Horn were too trusting about how the exchange would be reframed later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=2094" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:54</a> | Did he notice semantic games, tricks, or logical fallacies in that exchange? | He says there were semantic games around chattel slavery and a straw man about U.S. slavery, but not much coherent logic or exegesis to analyze. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=2315" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:35</a> | Does the Bible endorse slavery? | He says no; he frames the biblical laws as regulation of an existing ancient institution rather than endorsement of it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=2722" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:22</a> | What grade would Douglas Petrovich's dissertation have received? | He says he would have failed it because he considered it below standard. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=2765" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:05</a> | Are sacraments a means of salvation? | He says sacraments are important acts of faith and obedience, but Christ is the means of salvation, not the sacraments themselves. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=2975" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:35</a> | What does baptism do? | He presents baptism as initiation and a first act of repentance and obedience after committing oneself to Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=3151" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:31</a> | What would he say to those who call baptism a purely symbolic act? | He says it is not merely symbolic, but a necessary act predicated on faith and a next step in that faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=3299" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:59</a> | When did fundamentalism take root in America? | He places its rise in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries as a reactionary movement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=3811" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:31</a> | What was the Witch of Endor expecting in 1 Samuel 28? | He says she likely expected ordinary mediumistic activity, not Samuel himself, and her shock shows something unexpected happened. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=4103" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:23</a> | How should familiar spirits be understood? | He identifies them as demonic or fallen spiritual powers rather than harmless human spirits. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=4390" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:10</a> | Why did Moses go far from Midian to pasture his flocks? | He says the Sinai region had grasslands and Moses was following practical grazing conditions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=4476" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:36</a> | Has he heard anyone speak an "angelic tongue" that sounded like a real language? | He says no, despite hearing such claims. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=5120" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:20</a> | Were mummified rabbits ever a thing in ancient Egypt? | He says yes, though they are not as common as some other animal mummies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=5222" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:02</a> | Were the apostles likely teenagers? | He says yes; first-century disciples were often young men, commonly around ages 15 to 19, though some could be older or younger. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=5570" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:50</a> | What are his favorite ancient Egyptian graffiti examples? | He points to graffiti on abandoned temples, including Coptic marks and Egyptian humorous or crude graffiti, and says Egyptian graffiti was often funnier than Roman graffiti. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=5872" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:52</a> | What does "raised for our justification" mean in Romans 4:25? | He reads it as showing the completed work of Christ, contrasting being delivered up for transgressions with being raised for justification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=6100" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:40</a> | What criteria are used to number Egyptian dynasties and prevent them from being treated as co-regencies? | He says dynasties are judged by succession, blood relation, art or architectural change, kingdom domain, and other evidence; the sixth and thirteenth dynasties cannot be made parallel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=6580" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:40</a> | What does "like a dove" mean in Luke 3:22? | He says it is a simile for a visible bodily descent, and also evokes ancient Near Eastern bird imagery associated with kingship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=7100" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:20</a> | Who was the worst or most evil pharaoh? | He distinguishes incompetence from evil, naming Ptolemy XIII as very incompetent and pointing to Persian pharaohs as especially brutal. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=7446" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:06</a> | Did ancient Egyptians have an all-encompassing concept of God or fate above lesser gods, like the One in Neoplatonism? | He says no; Egyptian usage of "god" was usually contextual, with Atenist and later Amun-Re manifestation language being the closest parallels. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=7641" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:21</a> | Has his work ever made him question his faith? | He says no; his work has strengthened his faith, though he questioned his faith more as a teenager before entering that work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=8011" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:31</a> | What was the most decisive factor for victory in ancient Near Eastern warfare? | He answers logistics, emphasizing supply and practical organization. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=8245" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:25</a> | What rights did ancient Near Eastern women have, such as property or autonomy? | He says rights varied by culture, but Egyptian women had broad rights to work, earn income, own property, adopt, and enter or leave marriages. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=8399" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:59</a> | What academic study Bible would he recommend for the Old and New Testament? | He says there is not one best option for both testaments; he generally likes CSB and ESV study Bibles, especially archaeology-focused ones. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=8678" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:38</a> | Has he changed his mind that eating Christ's flesh and drinking his blood in John 6 is not about communion? | He says no; he still reads that passage as about commitment to Christ rather than directly about communion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=8842" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:22</a> | What little-known disturbing ancient Egypt facts does he like to tell people? | He gives examples involving mummification, including the smell and the way wet mummies can collapse into a mass of remains. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=9327" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:27</a> | What happens to those who have never heard the gospel or of Jesus Christ? | He says the issue is controversial, but argues they may be given a real encounter with Christ, while not being automatically saved or excluded. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=9601" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:01</a> | How did ancient Egyptians start fires? | He says they used both bow drills and flint, with bow drills more likely earlier and flint-and-steel methods later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=9688" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:28</a> | Is there evidence Hazor was rebuilt immediately after Joshua's destruction? | He says no; he reads Judges as referring to a king of Hazor rather than requiring a rebuilt city, using a transfer-of-flag idea. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=9954" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:54</a> | Is the Hazor transfer-of-flag idea like the Portuguese monarchy relocating to Brazil after Napoleon conquered Portugal? | He says yes, comparing both to an administrative or political identity moving to a different place. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=10037" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:17</a> | How can someone set up a podcast with him? | He says to contact him through Facebook or similar messaging, then he checks whether the conversation would be a good fit. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=10172" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:32</a> | Could some cryptids be possessed animals that mimic human voices or behave strangely? | He says it is possible in principle because Scripture has animals being possessed, but it is hard to prove and could also be ordinary animal behavior. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=10586" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:26</a> | If he lived in ancient Egypt, what would his occupation be? | He says he would probably be a metalsmith, because he can make knives and jewelry and could fit into that craft class. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=10835" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:35</a> | Has he seen Mike Winger's video on Benny Hinn, and what is his view of Hinn? | He says he has many opinions and describes Hinn as a long-standing false prophet and con man. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=11613" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:13:33</a> | Are there televangelists he thinks are genuine rather than grifters or heretics? | He says none come to mind, arguing that expensive television ministry tends to create pressure toward fundraising abuse. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=11900" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:20</a> | What ancient technologies would surprise people today? | He rejects weaponized mirrors but points to automata, mechanical birds, holy-water vending machines, and moving statues. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=12067" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:07</a> | Did Canaanites practice cannibalism? | He says there is slight evidence for one early Bronze Age context involving eating the flesh of the dead before burying bones under houses, but not broad evidence beyond that. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=12168" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:48</a> | Why is the New Revised Standard Version a default choice in academia? | He says it is often treated as the least religious translation, with a good critical base text and a more secular translation tendency. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=12277" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:37</a> | Is Kenneth Copeland a cryptid? | He treats it as a joke, then criticizes Copeland's wealth, estate, and private planes as inappropriate for a minister. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=12965" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:05</a> | Does Leviticus 25:44-46 apply only at Jubilee or always, and could foreign slaves be bought at any time? | He says foreign slaves were an exception to the Jubilee release rules, could be bought at any time, and the broader slavery questions remain debated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=13368" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:42:48</a> | What was the Lord thinking when he created the monkfish and called it good? | He jokes that the fish is ugly but very tasty, comparing it to lobster in flavor and texture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=13643" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:47:23</a> | When might his fiction series be available? | He says he has no estimate; two of three books are done, he still needs to finish the third and find a literary agent. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JA1A7IeLekE?t=13912" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:51:52</a> | Which is better for the city of Terah: Kesh or Urfa? | He strongly favors Kesh, citing linguistic, chronological, and biographical problems with the Urfa view and a close match between Terah and a known figure from Kesh. |
